title: My Long Weekend with Alpine Ops: 5 Reflections
author: Alpine Ops Guest
excerpt: Three days in the mountains with Alpine Ops turned a first serious multi-day trek into a lesson in health, care, safety, comfort, and company.

contentBlocks.content: I've hiked before, I've done a couple of treks. They've all been different experiences, but with one similarity: at some point of almost every adventure I have ambitiously undertaken, when things have started to feel difficult, there has been one thought that has gone through my head. It goes like this:
"How wholly unnecessary this is. I wouldn't be missing out on anything if I had just stayed home."

This time, though, was different. I still struggled a considerable amount (can you tell from the photo below?), but not once did I contemplate the idea of never having done this, my first "serious", multi-day trek, with Alpine Ops. Not even when I was drenched in the rain, ready to fall asleep, but unable to see the end of the path. Not even briefly. I knew as soon as I started that every bit of this trek was worth it - the flights, the drives, and the packing and prep.
The destination was, of course, also completely worth it. Just look at that top photo! It was so beautiful I have no words to do it justice, so that’s not what this blog is about.

Only three days in the mountains with Alpine Ops taught me a great deal, and that's what I'll write about instead. I've listed a few of my takeaways below. Some of them aren't necessarily new information, but what has been brought to the fore by my experiences from the weekend.
Here they are.
1. Health is wealth

This sounds like something my grandparents would say and I would roll my eyes at. But I suppose it's a cliché for a reason. And there's nothing like a physical challenge to make you remember and swear by it.

Health is everything, and it's so obvious, but we forget it sometimes! Often, like during this trek, I am forced to confront just how important my health and body are and how much more I should be doing for them. While I found myself laughing and joking in high spirits through the majority of the trek, it was in the last forty-five minutes of our ascent that I found my legs straining, my face clammy with dehydration (my fault for forgetting all the advice I was given to keep drinking water), my lungs threatening to give up, and worst of all, my lunch feeling like it was inching up my throat. All I wanted was to lie down and take a nap, so what kept me going?
Well, primarily, a team member of Alpine Ops who forced me to keep going, helped me with my rucksack, and didn't let me lag behind (thanks, Naveesh).

But also, the same thing that made me run all the way up a hill once in seven minutes just so I could see the ruins at the top before it closed in ten minutes. And the same thing that allows me to travel thirty hours at a stretch without breaking down, and lets me eat all the food I want without revolting against me, and protects me from getting sick. My body!

This trek reminded me to thank my body for keeping on doing its thing, for being on my side and for being dependable, for dutifully taking whatever responsibility I give it. I’ll eventually forget again and continue taking it for granted, but experiences such as this remind me to keep being grateful for my health and to treat my body well.
2. The magic is in the details

What does a base camp really need? A few tents, some food, a first-aid kit? That's what I thought. We arrived at our base camp with only these expectations, ready to eat Maggi for three days in a row, because what else were they going to give us up in the mountains?

contentBlocks.alt: Food and drinks arranged at base camp

contentBlocks.content: So imagine our surprise when we reached to find a table overlooking the most beautiful view, laid out with a bunch of beverages and condiments and a casserole full of biriyani. Biriyani! I think part of me suspected it was just a really great welcome to make a good first impression, but it remained consistent the whole time! Over the next couple of days at camp, we ate aloo parathas, soya wraps, rajma, noodles, and my favourite meal, Gobi manchurian and fried rice.

The food was just one aspect of it (but a great one!) Everything was so painstakingly arranged and every detail seemed so carefully thought through. Even the tents we stayed in were perfect for everything we needed and the gear we were each given helped so much. If I list all the stuff out, they'll all sound like small things, but they made a world of a difference.

contentBlocks.alt: Camp meal prepared during the Alpine Ops trek

contentBlocks.content: I'm just still impressed by the food and especially still thinking about the gulab jamun dessert we got after finishing our ascent to the lake and the DIY ginger-lemon-honey tea I drank at my last breakfast there.
3. Adventure and beauty can be more accessible than I thought
This one might be slightly deeper. It’s certainly one I think about a LOT.

I've lived and travelled all over the world. So while I know that our country has unmatched beauty and you can find nearly everything here that you can find anywhere else, I have always had one major problem: I don't think travel is as accessible here in India as it should be - especially for women. Mainly, it's the safety aspect.

I've enjoyed my freedom when I've traveled - traipsing around wherever I please without a second thought about who is around me or what I'm wearing. Here, in my own country, I haven't been able to do that so fully. I still do what I want, I roam around on my own, my (female) friends and I don't let fear stop us from wandering and adventuring and living the lives we want. But there is always that shadow of a question in the back of our minds about whether we are safe or not. We quickly cross streets when we see a shady-looking person or car on our side of it, or we joke about using what we learnt in a self-defence class if needed. It's not funny, but I guess joking is how we deal and make light of things.
So the complete absence of the thought of "What if something happens?" was so, so welcomed by us throughout this trek.

I arrived with some hesitation; I'd been convinced to go by my friend, and I was excited for the change of pace, but we were also hot off the heels of an unfortunate travel experience with a different operator, so I had some doubt in my mind about how this one would turn out.

My uncertainty dissipated the moment we met the Alpine Ops team. The first thing they assured us about was that we were in good hands and we shouldn't hesitate to tell them if there was anything we were concerned about or had questions about, since we were women traveling alone. We felt completely safe and trusted their judgment in the participants they brought along to the trek with us.

It was a liberating experience not having to worry about safety, and though we have a long way to go to make this kind of adventure and travel accessible for all women, this experience has certainly reinstated my faith that there are safe and reliable options for us.

contentBlocks.alt: Trekkers standing together in the mountains

contentBlocks.content: 3. My comfort zone is more flexible than I believed

I'm a creature of comfort. I'm told it's a Taurus trait. I live alone and everything in my home is tailored to my tastes, which means everything in it is conducive to maximum comfort, plenty of quality time with myself, and lots of space, which I am territorial about.

So as soon as I booked this trek, which was a week or so before I went on it, I started thinking about where I would sleep, how I would use the bio-toilets, how I would spend so much time constantly in close quarters with other people. I've only been on one-night camping trips before this. I didn't think I could do it for three nights, but peer pressure is a powerful thing, so I went along with the plan because my friend convinced me.

It was surprising how easily I took to everything once I arrived. Being squeezed together with my friends in a tent, scaring each other with horror stories and waking each other up to go to the bathroom took me back to boarding school, and everything felt like an adventure again. I was scared to peer into the toilet tent, not sure if I could use it, but I found myself just shrugging my hesitation away when I actually needed to. I was worried about not getting time to myself but that wasn't an issue either - I enjoyed the company of the people I was with and also had enough time to wander by myself around the camp and take in the sights.

It obviously helped that the team at Alpine Ops thought about everything, like I said. I usually equate camping with roughing it, giving up all my needs for a day or two, but it didn't even feel like I was really doing that because of how well-planned and set up everything was.

contentBlocks.alt: Alpine Ops campsite in the mountains

contentBlocks.content: 5. The destination < the journey < the company

I came prepared for this trek. (Okay, that's a lie. I only packed 1 glove - not sure how - no waterproof bottoms, and nothing to keep my ears warm. But I came prepared in my book, which means my earphones were fully charged and ready to use).

Trekking to me has been a solitary activity - navigating the terrain by myself while my brain is occupied with music or a Harry Potter audiobook. Turns out I never needed the earphones. I never even took them out of the pocket of my bag where I packed them. Again, props to the Alpine Ops team for bringing together a group of such great people. There were very few moments during which I wasn't laughing at the conversations we were having while climbing, or we weren't laughing at one another. We shared stories and experiences, and even when we were quiet and catching our breath or listening to the birds, it was all the more special because of the company.
I came across this illustration a few years ago and I'd forgotten the author and even the characters a long time ago, but the message stuck with me. I remembered it while writing this.

P.S. My apologies to everyone on the trek who had to listen to my laughing and giggling all day and also maybe through a good portion of the night. I just found everything funny all at once, for some reason.

contentBlocks.alt: Trekkers gathered together during the Alpine Ops weekend

contentBlocks.content: These are already a lot of reflections from three days, if you ask me, and I hope this is only the beginning. My friend Soumini, who enjoyed this trek as much as I did, said some places and experiences never leave you, and this is one of them for her. I agree. I am grateful to the social media algorithm because of which she stumbled upon Alpine Ops, very grateful to her for inviting me to go, and most grateful to the team itself - Raman and Ishan and everyone else - for taking such good care of us! It goes without saying that I'm already excited for more adventures with them.

title: Why Should You Embrace the Outdoors in 2026
author: Sudarshan Iyengar
excerpt: Inner peace isn't the only gift the mountains give. Sometimes, they give you a story you'll tell for the rest of your life.
content:
2017 June.
Parvati Valley.  

Wait, are we supposed to stay inside these tents? 
6 people, altogether? 
What did I even sign up for? 
This stupid trail. Rainy, muddy, terrible. 
My god, does this ever end? 

And, washrooms?
Let me not even think about it. 

Okay, bro, don’t EVER call me to another trek again. 
This is pure, unnecessary hell. 

Why would I pay my hard-earned money to suffer for 10 full days? 
Hell no! 

This was my daily rant on my first-ever Himalayan trek, summarised. 

As a naive 25-year-old who was accustomed to the comforts of life, I had grown to assume that all my luxuries were indeed necessities. 

During that time, a friend of mine wanted to surprise me by booking a 10-day trek to a mountain pass called Sar Pass in the Parvati Valley region of Himachal Pradesh. 

Needless to say, I hated everything about it, mainly because I never got MY WAY. 

One small week of discomfort had put me in my place. 
My meltdowns, every day of the trek, made my friends laugh and cringe, and I had vowed never to return. 

Fast forward to 2026 April, I have completed around 23 trips to the Himalayas, and can’t get enough of it. 

So, what changed?

Why, a regular guy like me, who is reasonably fit, active, naive, and young, would get addicted to something that he called ‘pure hell’? 

Let me unpack. 

If I have to explain myself to a person with a short attention span, I’d say just this:

After reading a few Navy SEAL and special forces novels, I was mentally yearning for the ‘feel’ of being a ‘badass outdoorsman’ or someone who is capable enough to live off the land. 

One week in the outdoors, I clearly understood how I totally overestimated myself.

But, once I went back, and started to go through every day in my mind, and browsed through every trek pic in our shared google drive, it set something off inside of me. 

The more I thought about it, the more I got happier and relaxed, visualizing everything that had conspired: The calm, the fresh mountain air, falling on my face during my first-ever snow walk, and laughing at the silliest of things at the numerous campsites. 

A voice inside of me kept asking me to revisit the Himalayas, just to investigate my own self… in case all these feelings were fake/made-up or something. 

The coming winter was a 15 day trip to the remotest regions of Ladakh in search of the most beautiful, yet most elusive being of all: The Snow leopard. 

The moment I saw one on my 14th day, from around 30 meters, I was sure of it all - Mountains are where I am my true, authentic self. I can be myself all I want, and nature will decide my course of action. 

That feeling of fearless openness, embracing vulnerability, and living every second of every day seemed unparalleled. 

That is when I decided: A minimum of 3 trips to the Himalayas, no matter what. 

Well, enough about me. You know my story. 

Here are 5 simple reasons why I feel you, the reader, SHOULD embrace the glorious outdoors, based on my own experiences. 

This is not a compulsion, rather a nudge to live a life less lived. 


Earn that Comfort 

Yes, sitting in an air conditioned chamber, working on your laptop all day, and going back to your comfortable, cozy home is a routine anyone can get accustomed to. 

I got so bored of comfort that I didn’t want anything else in life, and never was grateful to anything that was bestowed upon me. 

But, once I saw the other side, the long walks, the uncomfortable weather, the shared tents, drinking icy water with my shivering hands…and loving it all, made me appreciate all that I had back home. 

Every trip to the outdoors made me feel 1% happier than I was before, because, at home, I had running water, a bed to sleep in, a chair to sit on, and a breakfast that is cooked in a kitchen. 

Yes, a simple kitchen felt godsend, compared to how we at times cooked in the open, with the cold winds trying to play spoilsport all the while. 

Once I started ‘earning’ what was already mine, I became happier, more thankful, and grounded in all that I thought, spoke, and did. 

It had a tremendous effect on my life. 
Professional and personal. 

Don’t believe me? 
Do it yourself and let me know!

Take up a Challenge 

Every once a while, it actually is necessary to face a new challenge in life.

I know it sounds generic, but my only challenge in life was to reach my football ground from the office every day, before 8 PM. 

It does sound simple, but try covering 22 kms under 2 hours in Bangalore on a workday, and you’d see it yourself :) 

Once I started to trek, tasks like cooking dal-chawal under a rock by the river, getting a fire going when the temperature suddenly dropped to sub-zero, or just trying to zip-up my sleeping back without getting my beard stuck inside, all seemed like gargantuan challenges, which made me cherish the life outdoors, and made me only want it more. 

Accomplishing such simple-sounding tasks in the wilderness, gave me the much wanted confidence to navigate my work-tasks better, and to handle the stress that comes with it, even better. 

Who knew? 
Focusing on burning the collected firewood efficiently while cooking/stirring the rice carefully translated so well in multi-tasking at work! 

Grow a Little

I always thought this about growth in life. 

25-30: Work hard, get as many promotions as possible, make more money and enjoy life. 

30-40: Marry the girl of your dreams, take your children to football tournaments, and enjoy life. 

40-50: Invest all the money you’ve earned, grow it, and prepare for retirement. 

By 50 - Retire at 50, enjoy the fruits of your labor. 

I thought this, because that’s vaguely what everyone around me was doing. 

Happiness????
Ain’t nobody got time for that?!

Fast forward to now:

A life with full heart, millions of memories, friends (yes, friends, not colleagues or workmates) from every part of the country that are ready to take the next trip to the mountains: No questions asked. 

The friends you make on the trails, the souls with which you suffer and enjoy on the high mountains, will stay for life. 

This doesn’t mean all of this is some sort of spiritual BS without anything tangible. 

I have personally seen people meet people with similar life and work interests on the trails, make connections, create a community, and grow together in life and at work. 

In short: The growth I saw in the trekking trips seemed far more rewarding than anything I saw elsewhere. 

Maybe this could be the reason that could get you started? 

There is only one way to find out! 

Some Inner Peace

Yes, although this is a used/abused/overused word now, in its purest form, it still holds true. 

As someone who always prided himself in pressure/stressful situations, I did thrive in a high-expection work environment, I never knew how much of my headspace my work pressure was taking up. 

In the mountains, I ran into an elderly trekker from a different state who taught me a very simple thing, just as a part of our casual conversation around work-stress and decompression:
To observe my own self in second person. 

Once I started doing it, I understood how cluttered I was on the inside and what were the possible points of correction. Although it was a little difficult in the beginning, by the end of my 6th trek/trip, I felt remarkably calmer, more at peace with whatever that was happening around, and stopped chasing perfection. 

You cannot control your surroundings, you can just react to it. But, if you can control your reaction every time, you are halfway there!

Every trip I went up the mountains, I made a habit to get 1 percent closer than before, to stay mindful, peaceful, and less chaotic. 

It resulted in a happier, more grounded life back home and at work. 

Lesser arguments, fewer fights, less talking, more listening. Let life take its course. 

All of this was possible just by analysing my own personal behaviour, whilst being alone or under a rock or under a tree, after a long day of trek. 

In short: Yes, you can still achieve inner peace in the mountains in 2026! 

Evolve and Discover - There is More to Life! 

Inner peace isn't the only gift the mountains give. Sometimes, they give you a story you'll tell for the rest of your life.

On one of my treks in 2021, me and a friend had lost our path midway and strayed away from the trail on a morning run by almost 4-ish kilometers. 

Instead of going to a specific waterfall in Dharamkot, we had reached a small village called Naddi in Himachal, just by chance. 

In an effort to ask around for location the traditional way (our phones had no network), we found an old mountain shepherd, who took us to his house, gave us chai, shared a few stories, and even arranged for a jeep transport back to the local bus stand! 

Although I’d never wish you, the reader, to get lost on the trails, I’d definitely be happy if you took a chance on yourself, and take the off-beat path every once in a while. 

Life on the other side, people on the other side, and the experience of living care-free on the other side, are all worth their weight in gold, when all is said and done. 

Take that first step, go out, explore, and evolve. 

You will see a different version of yourself when you are back home every time!

These diverse experiences will make you a few thousand rupees/dollars poorer every time, but make you richer in memories and LIFE, by a lot. 

So, plan something, take that trip you always wanted, go to the mountains, walk on ridgelines, drink the glacier water, spot wildlife in the wild, and you will come back with an urge to go back! 

Happy Outdoors! 

If you wish to take your first step into the mountains and wilderness with a team of highly experienced mountaineers, you can contact Alpine Ops.
